symbols: bodies rising from grass — the general resurrection of the dead
garden flowers — paradise restored, the new creation
stretching and yawning figures — resurrection as a gentle awakening from sleep
village setting — the ordinary world transformed by the resurrection
description: A scene of multiple fleshy, rounded human figures lying and rising from a lush green garden filled with flowers. The figures appear to be waking up — stretching, yawning, sitting up — in various states of undress. Some women in patterned dresses look on. Children and adults are intermingled. The setting is a garden with abundant wildflowers and greenery. The style is distinctively Spencer — rounded, tactile bodies in an everyday English landscape, with bright, saturated colors and a flattened, folk-art quality.
theological_description: Stanley Spencer's visionary painting reimagines the general resurrection as a gentle awakening in the familiar setting of his beloved village of Cookham. Rather than a terrifying apocalyptic event, Spencer depicts resurrection as waking from sleep — echoing Jesus' words about the dead girl: 'She is not dead but sleeping' (Mark 5:39). Paul described death as sleep: 'We shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed' (1 Cor 15:51). The garden setting recalls both Eden and the eschatological paradise, where 'he will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more' (Rev 21:4). The ordinariness of the scene — everyday people in a village garden — expresses Spencer's theology that the resurrection transforms the mundane into the sacred. Paul's great resurrection chapter provides the theological foundation: 'What is sown in dishonor is raised in glory... It is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body' (1 Cor 15:43-44). Spencer believed heaven would be Cookham on a summer day, but with everyone fully alive.

scripture_refs.reason: We shall not all sleep but we shall all be changed — the awakening from death
Sown in dishonor, raised in glory — the resurrected bodies waking
Death shall be no more — the gentle, peaceful resurrection scene
She is not dead but sleeping — death as sleep from which one wakes
